nativeRange.habitat: Sandy shoreline shallows of Lake Tanganyika, particularly areas where empty shells of Neothauma tanganyicense snails accumulate

decorPreferences: minimal tall decor — low-profile rocks acceptable as territory dividers

diet.notes: Multifasciatus Shell Dwellers are carnivores that accept a range of small live and frozen foods readily. Frozen daphnia, baby brine shrimp, cyclops, and bloodworms are all well-accepted, as are crushed cichlid pellets and nano-sized sinking pellets. In the wild, colonies tend to feed on plankton drifting through the water column above their shell beds.

goodWith: rock-dwelling Tanganyikans (Julies, Altolamprologus) in larger tanks
species not competing for bottom/sand space
avoidWith: boisterous or large cichlids that disturb shells

imageCandidates.notes: Richard A. Ingebrigtsen; 4272x2848. Sharp, well-lit group of three (dominant male plus two smaller individuals) showing the fine many-banded vertical striping, iridescent eye and colony behaviour of this shell-dwelling species.
NasserHalaweh; 1342x1007. Clean crisp lateral profile of a single adult, whole fish in frame, clearly displaying the numerous narrow vertical bars.

Richard Andre Ingebrigtsen; 1632x1224. Individual perched among a bed of empty snail shells, illustrating the defining shell-dweller habitat; fish is paler/lighter-coloured, kept as habitat-context extra.

summary: Widely considered one of the smallest cichlid species in the world, the Multifasciatus Shell Dweller is endemic to Lake Tanganyika, where colonies live in and around empty snail shells. It breeds readily in captivity and displays bold territorial behavior despite its tiny size.
careNotes: Generally best kept as a colony of 6+ fish with at least 2–3 females per male. A minimum of 20 empty snail shells is commonly recommended — Escargot snail shells (cooked and thoroughly cleaned) are widely reported as an excellent substitute for the native Neothauma tanganyicense shells. Shells should be distributed across a sand floor at least 5 cm deep. Each fish tends to claim its own shell and vigorously defends a small territory around it; despite their tiny size, Multifasciatus Shell Dwellers have been reported to be surprisingly bold and assertive in that defense. Rift Lake water chemistry is important: hard, alkaline water in the pH 7.8–9.0 range is generally advised, maintained with crushed coral or aragonite sand substrate. Species-only setups or Tanganyikan biotope tanks with open-water species are usually recommended to avoid shell disturbance. Males are noticeably larger than females, reaching 4–4.5 cm compared to around 3 cm for females. Two closely related shell-dwelling species — Neolamprologus similis (the similis shell dweller) and Neolamprologus ocellatus (the ocellatus shell dweller) — share similar care requirements and are sometimes kept alongside or instead of this species.
breedingNotes: Breeding is generally considered easy and often occurs without special intervention once a colony is established. Females deposit eggs inside their chosen shell, and males fertilize from outside. The female tends the eggs and guards the fry within the shell for the first week or two. Multiple females in a colony typically breed simultaneously. Fry are usually large enough to accept baby brine shrimp or microworms once free-swimming. Parents have been known to remain tolerant of growing fry within the colony, though older juveniles may eventually need to be removed as the colony expands and shell competition increases.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Shell-dweller care guide covering N. multifasciatus. Tank size recommendation 10–20 gal; this entry uses 75L (20 gal long) as minimum appropriate for a colony per user brief and SeriouslyFish guidance.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichliformes), size (4.0 cm max), depth range (6–12 m), native range (Lake Tanganyika endemic), and water parameters. Size across sources: SeriouslyFish 4.5 cm males / 3.5 cm females; FishBase 4.0 cm; user brief 4 cm males / 3 cm females. Used 3.0–4.5 cm range.

fish.breedingNotes: The Multifasciatus Shell Dweller is a shell brooder: the female deposits her eggs inside a chosen snail shell, and spawning tends to occur readily in a well-maintained colony where adequate shells are available.
